content.text: Latency is the time between sending a request to an LLM API and receiving the response. For interactive applications — chat, copilots, voice — latency is not a secondary concern but a primary usability constraint. A model that produces a better answer in five seconds may be worse than one that produces a good-enough answer in one second if users are waiting for each turn. Understanding the anatomy of LLM latency, what drives it, and how to measure and reduce it gives you concrete levers to pull when tuning your application.
Latency Anatomy: TTFT, TBT, and E2E

attrs.alt: LLM latency anatomy: network-out, queue/scheduling, prefill (prompt processing) up to TTFT, then decode (token-by-token generation) measuring TBT, then network-back to total E2E. Key drivers: model size, context length, output length, infra/batching. Streaming makes TTFT the perceived latency.

content.text: LLM latency breaks into three phases that map to different user experiences and optimization strategies:

— time from request dispatch to receiving the first token. Covers network round-trip, server queue, and prompt prefill (processing all input tokens into a KV cache). This is the "felt" latency for streaming UIs — the pause before text starts appearing.

— time to generate each successive token. Each token requires one forward pass through the model; TBT determines streaming "smoothness." A 50 ms TBT gives 20 tokens/second — fast enough for reading; 200 ms gives 5 tokens/second — visibly stuttering.
— total wall-clock time from sending the request to receiving the last token. E2E = TTFT + (TBT × output_tokens). Relevant for batch jobs, offline pipelines, and evaluating throughput.

Streaming does not reduce total latency — it changes which latency users perceive. With streaming, the felt delay is TTFT (often 0.3–1s). Without streaming, the felt delay is E2E (often 5–30s). Enable streaming for all interactive use cases.
More parameters = more compute per token. A 70B model is ~7× slower per token than a 7B at same hardware.
Choose smaller model for latency-critical paths; use frontier only where capability gap is measurable.
Prefill is O(n) in input tokens. A 10k-token prompt prefills ~10× slower than 1k tokens. Attention is quadratic in transformers but prefill is usually parallelised across GPUs.
Trim system prompts; cache repeated large contexts with prompt caching APIs.
Each output token requires one forward pass. 500-token response takes 500× longer to decode than 1 token.
Set max_tokens tightly; instruct model to be concise for latency-critical tasks.
Providers batch multiple requests onto the same GPU pass for throughput. High server load → your request batched with many others → individual TBT increases.
Use dedicated capacity / reserved throughput for SLA-sensitive workloads.
Physical RTT from client to model datacenter. Cross-continent adds 80–200 ms each way.
Provision in region closest to users; use provider region selection APIs.
Under load, requests wait in provider queue before GPU assignment. Can add 0.5–5 s at peak traffic.
Monitor queue position via provider dashboards; shift batch work to off-peak or async paths.
Measure TTFT and TBT separately — they tell different stories. TTFT is observable by recording the timestamp of the first streaming chunk; TBT is the gap between successive chunks.

import time
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def measure_latency(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 256) -> dict:
    """Returns TTFT, mean TBT, E2E, and token count."""
    t_send = time.perf_counter()
    t_first: float | None = None
    chunk_times: list[float] = []
    token_count = 0

    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            now = time.perf_counter()
            if t_first is None:
                t_first = now
            else:
                chunk_times.append(now - chunk_times[-1] if chunk_times else now - t_first)
            chunk_times_abs = chunk_times  # store for mean TBT
            token_count += 1  # approximate: one chunk ≈ one token

    t_end = time.perf_counter()

    ttft = (t_first - t_send) if t_first else None
    mean_tbt = sum(chunk_times) / len(chunk_times) if chunk_times else None
    e2e = t_end - t_send

    return {
        "ttft_s": round(ttft, 3) if ttft else None,
        "mean_tbt_ms": round(mean_tbt * 1000, 1) if mean_tbt else None,
        "e2e_s": round(e2e, 3),
        "tokens_approx": token_count,
        "tokens_per_sec": round(token_count / e2e, 1),
    }

result = measure_latency("Explain how transformers work in three sentences.")
print(result)
# {'ttft_s': 0.421, 'mean_tbt_ms': 23.4, 'e2e_s': 3.87, 'tokens_approx': 64, 'tokens_per_sec': 16.5}
A single measurement is misleading. LLM latency has high variance due to server load, batching, and KV cache state. Run at least 20 samples and report p50, p90, p99 — the tail matters for UX.
Real-time chat, intent routing, high-volume pipelines
Production assistants, code review, document Q&A
Deep reasoning, complex agents — not interactive latency-critical

These are approximate ranges observed at moderate server load. Published numbers drift with infrastructure updates. Always benchmark your specific workload at the context length you use in production — a 512-token prompt behaves very differently from a 10k-token one.
The single highest-ROI latency change for chat applications. Show output as it streams instead of waiting for the complete response.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def stream_to_user(user_message: str) -> str:
    """Print tokens as they arrive; return full text."""
    collected = []
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            print(text, end="", flush=True)  # user sees each token immediately
            collected.append(text)
    print()  # newline after response
    return "".join(collected)
2. Prompt Caching to Reduce Prefill Time

Anthropic's prompt caching lets you mark a long, stable prefix (e.g., a large system prompt or retrieved document) for server-side caching. Subsequent requests with the same prefix skip prefill for that segment, dramatically cutting TTFT for long-context applications.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LARGE_DOCUMENT = "... 50,000 words of reference text ..."  # loaded once

def ask_about_document(question: str) -> str:
    """Uses prompt caching: first call caches the document; subsequent calls skip prefill."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": "You are a research assistant. Answer questions based on the document provided.",
            },
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": LARGE_DOCUMENT,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # mark for caching
            },
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    # Check cache hit in usage: response.usage.cache_read_input_tokens > 0 on cache hit
    return response.content[0].text

# First call: caches document → slow prefill
# Calls 2+: document read from cache → fast TTFT

E2E latency scales linearly with output tokens. If your application only needs short structured outputs (a JSON object, a classification label, a yes/no decision), setting a low max_tokens cap is a direct multiplier on speed.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def classify_intent(user_message: str) -> str:
    """Fast intent classification with tight output budget."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=16,          # classification only needs a few tokens
        system="""Classify the user's intent. Reply with exactly one word from:
search | create | delete | update | help | other""",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip().lower()

# At max_tokens=16, this completes in ~400 ms vs 2+ s for an open-ended response
intent = classify_intent("Can you help me find my last order?")
print(intent)  # "search"

When a workflow requires multiple independent LLM calls (e.g., retrieve several pieces of information, then synthesize), run them concurrently. The wall-clock time is then the slowest single call, not their sum.

import asyncio
import anthropic

async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

async def classify_async(text: str, category: str) -> dict:
    response = await async_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=32,
        system=f"Is the following text about {category}? Reply yes or no.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return {"category": category, "answer": response.content[0].text.strip().lower()}

async def classify_all(text: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Run 4 classification checks concurrently — takes as long as ONE call."""
    categories = ["finance", "technology", "health", "politics"]
    tasks = [classify_async(text, cat) for cat in categories]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

results = asyncio.run(classify_all("The Fed raised interest rates by 25bp."))
print(results)
# [{'category': 'finance', 'answer': 'yes'},
#  {'category': 'technology', 'answer': 'no'}, ...]

Route easy requests to a fast small model and only escalate to a slower frontier model when the fast model expresses low confidence or fails a validation check. The majority of requests complete at low latency.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FAST_MODEL = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"  # TTFT ~400ms
SLOW_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-6"          # TTFT ~800ms

def answer_with_cascade(question: str) -> str:
    # First attempt: fast model with confidence signal
    fast_response = client.messages.create(
        model=FAST_MODEL,
        max_tokens=256,
        system="""Answer the question. If uncertain, start your response with [LOW_CONFIDENCE].
        Only add this tag if you genuinely are not confident.""",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    answer = fast_response.content[0].text

    if "[LOW_CONFIDENCE]" in answer:
        # Escalate to slower, more capable model
        slow_response = client.messages.create(
            model=SLOW_MODEL,
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
        )
        return slow_response.content[0].text

    return answer.replace("[LOW_CONFIDENCE]", "").strip()

# Simple factual question → fast model responds in ~500ms
print(answer_with_cascade("What is the capital of France?"))
# Nuanced edge case → fast model says LOW_CONFIDENCE → escalated to Sonnet
When Latency Is (and Isn't) the Right Metric
User experiences the wait before first token; rest streams naturally
Suggestion must appear before user finishes typing the next character
User reads while it streams; stutter is more annoying than a long TTFT
No user waiting; optimise for total job time with concurrency, not per-request latency
User waits for the complete agent output, not individual tokens; per-call latency compounds across many steps

Define a latency SLO before shipping to production. A common pattern is "p90 TTFT < 1 s for interactive, p99 E2E < 30 s for batch." Instrument every production call and alert when the moving p90 exceeds your target.

import time
import statistics
from collections import deque
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class LatencyMonitor:
    """Rolling window latency monitor with SLO alerting."""

    def __init__(self, window: int = 100, ttft_p90_slo: float = 1.0):
        self.ttfts: deque[float] = deque(maxlen=window)
        self.slo = ttft_p90_slo

    def record(self, ttft: float) -> None:
        self.ttfts.append(ttft)
        if len(self.ttfts) >= 20:  # enough data to compute percentile
            p90 = sorted(self.ttfts)[int(len(self.ttfts) * 0.9)]
            if p90 > self.slo:
                print(f"SLO BREACH: p90 TTFT {p90:.2f}s > {self.slo}s")

monitor = LatencyMonitor(window=100, ttft_p90_slo=1.0)

def monitored_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    t_first = None
    chunks = []

    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            if t_first is None:
                t_first = time.perf_counter()
                monitor.record(t_first - t0)
            chunks.append(text)

    return "".join(chunks)
Measuring non-streaming E2E and assuming that reflects user experience.
Users with streaming enabled feel TTFT, not E2E. Benchmark with the same transport mode the app uses.
Variance is high. Always report p50/p90/p99 across at least 20 runs at realistic prompt lengths.
Benchmarking with short prompts in production with long ones.
Prefill time scales with input tokens. Benchmark at the context length your users actually send.
Ignoring provider dashboard status during benchmarks.
A benchmark run during provider incident gives unrepresentative high latency numbers that don't reflect normal SLOs.
Optimising latency when throughput is the actual bottleneck.
For batch jobs, concurrent requests (not lower per-request latency) is the lever. Distinguish interactive vs batch requirements before choosing an optimisation strategy.
